subtopic_detail: 3-monthly slit-lamp screening in ANA-positive oligoarticular JIA
stem: You are the paediatric rheumatology registrar reviewing a 4-year-old girl with a 6-week history of intermittent painless right knee swelling and mild morning stiffness in the same joint. She has no fever, rash, mouth ulcers, weight loss, or systemic features. Examination shows a swollen non-tender right knee with reduced flexion, no other joints involved, and no enthesitis. Inflammatory markers are mildly elevated, ANA is positive at 1:320, RF and HLA-B27 are negative, and FBC is normal. Oligoarticular JIA has been diagnosed and an intra-articular triamcinolone injection has been planned. The family asks what additional screening their daughter requires.

data_table.Examination: Swollen non-tender right knee with reduced flexion, no other joints involved, no rash, no enthesitis, no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: ANA 1:320 positive, RF negative, HLA-B27 negative, ESR 32, CRP 18, FBC normal, MRI knee shows synovitis with effusion, no slit-lamp examination done yet

lead_in: What additional screening should be recommended?

options.text: Annual review by an ophthalmologist with formal visual acuity testing and fundoscopy alone, with patient and family education to seek earlier review if any visual symptoms such as redness, pain, or photophobia occur in either eye
options.rationale: Annual screening is insufficient frequency for high-risk patients (ANA-positive oligoarticular JIA in young girls); chronic anterior uveitis is typically asymptomatic until visual loss has occurred. (Source: RCH CPG: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis)

options.text: Testing for HLA-B27 has already been performed and was negative so further specific eye screening is not indicated, with reassurance that uveitis only complicates HLA-B27-positive enthesitis-related JIA
options.rationale: HLA-B27 is associated with acute symptomatic uveitis (enthesitis-related arthritis) but the highest absolute risk of chronic asymptomatic uveitis is in ANA-positive oligoarticular JIA. (Source: RCH CPG: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis)

options.text: Six-monthly review by an orthopaedic surgeon for joint protection advice and assessment of leg-length discrepancy, with no requirement for additional eye review beyond standard school vision screening at age five
options.rationale: Orthopaedic review may be appropriate for monitoring leg-length discrepancy from chronic knee inflammation but does not address the high uveitis risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis)

options.text: Three-monthly slit-lamp examination by an ophthalmologist to screen for chronic anterior uveitis, given that ANA-positive oligoarticular JIA in young girls is the highest-risk category and uveitis is typically asymptomatic until visual loss occurs
options.rationale: ANA-positive oligoarticular JIA in girls under 7 carries the highest risk of chronic asymptomatic anterior uveitis; 3-monthly slit-lamp screening is standard during the first years after diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis)

options.text: Reassure the family that screening for uveitis is only required if the patient develops symptoms of redness, pain or visual loss, given the low risk in oligoarticular JIA compared with polyarticular and systemic onset disease
options.rationale: Chronic anterior uveitis in oligoarticular JIA is characteristically asymptomatic; waiting for symptoms allows synechiae, cataract, glaucoma and visual loss to occur. (Source: RCH CPG: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis)

explanation.summary: Chronic anterior uveitis is the most important extra-articular complication of JIA. The highest risk is in young (under 7), ANA-positive, oligoarticular JIA - up to 30 percent develop uveitis within the first 4 years. Screening frequency is determined by age at diagnosis, subtype and ANA status. For high-risk children, 3-monthly slit-lamp examination by an ophthalmologist is recommended for the first 4 years, then 6-monthly, then annually. Uveitis is typically asymptomatic; early detection prevents synechiae, cataract, glaucoma and visual loss.
key_points: Highest uveitis risk: ANA-positive oligoarticular JIA in girls under 7
3-monthly slit-lamp for first 4 years in high-risk category
Symptoms are absent until complications develop; screen don't wait
explanation.pearls: First-line uveitis treatment is topical steroid and mydriatic; refractory cases require methotrexate or adalimumab via paediatric rheumatology coordination with ophthalmology.

subtopic_detail: Levodopa first-line for symptomatic functional impact in older-onset Parkinson disease
stem:
You are the general physician reviewing a 68-year-old retired accountant referred by his GP with 12 months of progressive bradykinesia, an asymmetric right-hand rest tremor, micrographia, and stiffness most evident on his right side. Examination shows cogwheel rigidity at the right wrist, reduced right-arm swing, no postural instability, normal vertical eye movements, and no cerebellar signs. An empirical low-dose levodopa challenge produced a 50 percent reduction in his UPDRS motor score.

He is functionally impaired (struggling with shoelaces and handwriting his usual diary entries), has no cognitive impairment on MoCA 27/30, and has no orthostatic, urinary or REM-sleep behavioural symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Asymmetric right rest tremor, cogwheel rigidity right wrist, micrographia, reduced right arm swing, no postural instability, normal vertical eye movements, no cerebellar signs
data_table.Investigations: Levodopa challenge produced 50% UPDRS motor improvement, MRI brain unremarkable for age, B12 and TSH normal, no medication-induced cause identified

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Rasagiline 1 mg daily as monotherapy MAO-B inhibitor providing modest symptomatic benefit and good tolerability for early Parkinson disease with minimal functional impact, deferring levodopa until functional decline progresses further
options.rationale: MAO-B inhibitor monotherapy is for very mild disease with minimal functional impact; this patient has clear functional impairment warranting more effective symptom control. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Neurology: Parkinson disease)

options.text: Pramipexole 0.125 mg three times daily titrated weekly as dopamine agonist monotherapy in order to delay levodopa exposure and reduce the long-term risk of dyskinesia, with monitoring for impulse control disorder and somnolence
options.rationale: Dopamine agonists carry significant impulse control disorder, somnolence, oedema and hallucination risk especially in older men, and are less effective than levodopa for motor symptoms. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Neurology: Parkinson disease)

options.text: Amantadine 100 mg twice daily as monotherapy for symptomatic improvement of bradykinesia and tremor in early Parkinson disease, with later addition of levodopa if symptoms progress beyond what can be controlled by amantadine alone
options.rationale: Amantadine has only modest motor benefit and is more often used as add-on for dyskinesia later in disease; it is not an appropriate sole first-line agent for functionally impairing PD. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Neurology: Parkinson disease)

options.text: Defer all pharmacotherapy and refer to a physiotherapist and occupational therapist with movement disorders interest for tailored exercise program, with neurology review in 6 months to reassess and consider levodopa at that time only if needed
options.rationale: Exercise is an important adjunct but does not control functionally impairing motor symptoms; effective drug therapy should be commenced now given functional impact. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Neurology: Parkinson disease)

options.text: Levodopa-carbidopa 100/25 mg three times daily titrated to symptoms as first-line therapy, given functional impact, older age, and good response to levodopa challenge, with monitoring for nausea, orthostasis, and later motor fluctuations
options.rationale: Levodopa is the most effective Parkinson disease therapy and is first-line for older patients (over 65-70) with functional impact, where motor fluctuation and dyskinesia risks are lower than in younger patients. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Neurology: Parkinson disease)

explanation.summary: Initial drug therapy in Parkinson disease depends on patient age, symptom severity, and functional impact. Levodopa remains the most effective agent and is first-line in older patients (typically over 65-70) with functional impairment, because they are less likely to develop motor fluctuations and dyskinesia and more vulnerable to dopamine agonist adverse effects. Dopamine agonists are preferred initial therapy only in younger patients with mild symptoms. MAO-B inhibitors and amantadine are options for very mild disease.
key_points: Levodopa: most effective; first-line in older patients with functional impact
Dopamine agonists: impulse control disorder, somnolence, hallucinations - caution in elderly
Exercise (especially boxing/tai-chi/Lee Silverman big-and-loud) is adjunctive
explanation.pearls: PIGD-predominant atypical features (early postural instability and falls, gaze palsy) suggest PSP/MSA rather than idiopathic PD; absence of vertical gaze palsy here is reassuring.

sources.label: NICE NG71: Parkinson's disease in adults

subtopic_detail: Continuing lithium in pregnancy for bipolar I with manic relapse history
stem:
You are the perinatal psychiatry registrar providing pre-conception counselling to a 32-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder, well controlled on lithium 750 mg nocte for the past 5 years with no mood episodes during this time. Her recent lithium level is 0.8 mmol/L, eGFR 90, TSH 2.4, corrected calcium normal. Three years ago she self-discontinued lithium for 6 weeks because of weight concerns and developed a severe manic episode requiring involuntary admission with psychosis.

She has had two earlier hospitalised manic episodes pre-lithium. She and her partner plan to commence trying to conceive within 2 months and she asks for advice on her medication during pregnancy.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic, no current suicidal ideation, no psychotic symptoms, insightful, motivated for pregnancy planning
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 0.8 mmol/L, eGFR 90, TSH 2.4, corrected calcium 2.40, FBC and UEC normal, urine bHCG negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pre-conception plan for her mood stabiliser?

options.text: Switch immediately to lamotrigine 25 mg daily with planned titration to 200 mg over 6 weeks as a safer mood stabiliser during conception and pregnancy, since lamotrigine has the most reassuring safety profile in pregnancy
options.rationale: Lamotrigine is more effective for depressive than manic episodes; her manic-predominant phenotype with severe manic relapses makes lamotrigine inadequate prophylaxis. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Perinatal Mental Health

options.text: Switch to sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily for the duration of pregnancy and the postpartum period, accepting some teratogenic risk in exchange for superior mood stabilisation across both manic and depressive episodes
options.rationale: Valproate has the highest teratogenic risk of any mood stabiliser (around 10 percent major congenital malformations plus neurodevelopmental risk) and is contraindicated in women planning pregnancy. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Perinatal Mental Health

options.text: Continue lithium at the lowest effective dose throughout pregnancy with foetal echocardiography at 18-20 weeks, monthly lithium levels with rising frequency near term, hydration counselling, and planned dose adjustment around delivery to avoid toxicity
options.rationale: Continuing lithium is appropriate given high relapse risk off lithium; absolute Ebstein anomaly risk is small (around 1 in 1000) and is screened with foetal echocardiography, with maternal benefit outweighing foetal risk in manic-predominant disease. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Perinatal Mental Health

options.text: Stop lithium abruptly at confirmation of pregnancy and restart only at the beginning of the second trimester after organogenesis is complete, with close monitoring for early mood relapse and re-titration of lithium dose at that time
options.rationale: First-trimester relapse risk is substantially higher than Ebstein risk; abrupt discontinuation also markedly increases relapse risk and has been associated with reduced response when lithium is restarted. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Perinatal Mental Health

options.text: Switch to quetiapine 300 mg nocte as a non-teratogenic atypical antipsychotic with mood-stabilising effect, with cross-titration off lithium over 4 weeks prior to conception and ongoing monitoring for metabolic adverse effects in pregnancy
options.rationale: Quetiapine is a reasonable alternative when lithium cannot be continued, but switching introduces relapse risk unnecessarily in a patient already well-controlled with severe manic recurrence history. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Perinatal Mental Health

explanation.summary: Lithium in early pregnancy is associated with a small absolute increase in Ebstein anomaly (background risk roughly 1 in 20,000; with first-trimester lithium roughly 1 in 1000-2000). This is detectable by foetal echocardiography at 18-20 weeks. In a patient with severe manic-predominant bipolar I and recent relapse off lithium, the maternal relapse risk on withdrawal far outweighs the small foetal cardiac risk. Continue lithium at the lowest effective dose, monitor levels monthly (then more frequently approaching term), maintain hydration, and reduce dose around delivery to avoid toxicity from volume contraction.
key_points: Continue lithium when manic-predominant illness and high relapse risk
Ebstein risk ~1:1000 with first-trimester lithium - screen at 18-20 weeks
Valproate contraindicated in women of childbearing age (10% major malformations)
explanation.pearls: Lithium clearance increases in second and third trimesters then plummets after delivery; check levels weekly in final weeks and reduce or hold dose at labour onset to avoid maternal toxicity post-partum.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Perinatal Mental Health
COPE Perinatal Mental Health Guidelines: Bipolar disorder in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Low-dose vaginal oestrogen for urogenital atrophy after early breast cancer
stem:
You are the GP reviewing a 56-year-old woman with troublesome vaginal dryness, dyspareunia significantly affecting her relationship, and three culture-confirmed urinary tract infections over the past 6 months attributed to urogenital atrophy. She completed treatment for ER-positive early breast cancer 8 years ago (lumpectomy, radiotherapy, 5 years of tamoxifen) and is currently on no breast cancer therapy with no recurrence on annual surveillance.

She has trialled non-hormonal vaginal lubricants and moisturisers consistently over the past 6 months without benefit. She does not have significant systemic vasomotor symptoms and is otherwise well.

data_table.Examination: Atrophic vaginal mucosa with thinning of rugae, pale and friable epithelium, no obvious lesion, no pelvic mass; breast examination unchanged from baseline
data_table.Investigations: Recent surveillance mammogram normal, no current oncological therapy, eGFR >90, vaginal pH 6.5, urine culture pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Commence systemic combined hormone therapy with oral conjugated equine oestrogen 0.625 mg plus medroxyprogesterone acetate 2.5 mg daily for symptom control of both urogenital and vasomotor symptoms, with annual mammogram surveillance
options.rationale: Systemic combined HRT is contraindicated in women with prior ER-positive breast cancer due to recurrence risk; even if vasomotor symptoms were predominant other agents would be preferred. (Source: AMS Position Statement: HRT in Breast Cancer Survivors)
source_refs: AMS Position Statement: HRT in Breast Cancer Survivors

options.text: Continue intensive non-hormonal lubricants and moisturisers with sexual health counselling and referral to a pelvic floor physiotherapist for assessment of pelvic floor function, with reassurance that hormonal therapy is not safe
options.rationale: She has trialled non-hormonal therapy adequately without benefit; continuing the same approach indefinitely is inadequate when refractory urogenital atrophy is causing recurrent UTIs and dyspareunia. (Source: AMS Position Statement: HRT in Breast Cancer Survivors)
source_refs: AMS Position Statement: HRT in Breast Cancer Survivors

options.text: Trial low-dose vaginal oestradiol pessary 10 mcg twice weekly in consultation with her breast oncology team, with patient counselling regarding the small but uncertain risk and the option to reassess after 3 to 6 months of therapy
options.rationale: Low-dose vaginal oestrogen has minimal systemic absorption and is considered an option for severe refractory urogenital atrophy after early breast cancer, after shared decision-making with the oncology team. (Source: AMS Position Statement: HRT in Breast Cancer Survivors)
source_refs: AMS Position Statement: HRT in Breast Cancer Survivors

options.text: Commence ospemifene 60 mg daily as a selective oestrogen receptor modulator with vaginal trophic effect and no systemic oestrogenic activity, providing an effective alternative to vaginal oestrogen in breast cancer survivors
options.rationale: Ospemifene is contraindicated in women with active breast cancer and not recommended after breast cancer in Australian guidance due to insufficient safety data. (Source: AMS Position Statement: HRT in Breast Cancer Survivors)
source_refs: AMS Position Statement: HRT in Breast Cancer Survivors

options.text: Commence intravaginal DHEA (prasterone) 6.5 mg daily as a tissue-specific androgenic precursor with minimal systemic effect, since the intravaginal route bypasses systemic exposure and is therefore safer in breast cancer survivors
options.rationale: Intravaginal prasterone has limited safety data in breast cancer survivors and is not first-line; vaginal oestrogen at low dose has more established data with shared oncology decision-making. (Source: AMS Position Statement: HRT in Breast Cancer Survivors)
source_refs: AMS Position Statement: HRT in Breast Cancer Survivors

explanation.summary: Urogenital atrophy after breast cancer is common and often refractory to non-hormonal therapy. Low-dose vaginal oestrogen (estradiol pessary 10 mcg twice weekly or oestriol cream) has minimal systemic absorption and observational data support safety in women with prior ER-positive disease, particularly after non-hormonal therapy has failed. The decision should be shared with the breast oncology team and the woman counselled regarding the small uncertain risk. Systemic HRT and ospemifene are not used after breast cancer.
key_points: Refractory urogenital atrophy post-BC: low-dose vaginal oestrogen with oncology input
Systemic HRT contraindicated after ER+ breast cancer
Non-hormonal first; escalate to vaginal oestrogen if inadequate
explanation.pearls: Women on aromatase inhibitors may have greater systemic absorption concerns from vaginal oestrogen than those off oncological therapy; oncology team input is essential.

sources.label: AMS Position Statement: HRT in Breast Cancer Survivors

subtopic_detail: Observation only for paediatric ITP without major bleeding
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 5-year-old previously well girl referred by the GP with 5 days of widespread petechiae and easy bruising, and a viral URTI 2 weeks ago. Examination shows widespread petechiae and ecchymoses but no mucosal bleeding, no haematuria, no haematochezia, no organomegaly, no lymphadenopathy and no bony tenderness. She is alert, playing happily, and has had no head trauma. The blood film shows isolated thrombocytopenia with normal red and white cell morphology and no blasts. Acute immune thrombocytopenia is diagnosed.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9, HR 102, BP 96/60, SpO2 99% room air, GCS 15
data_table.Examination: Widespread petechiae and ecchymoses, no mucosal bleeding, no haematuria, no haematochezia, no organomegaly, no lymphadenopathy, no bony tenderness, playful
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 12, Hb 130, WCC 7.0 with normal differential, blood film isolated thrombocytopenia with no blasts, reticulocytes normal, coagulation profile normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Single dose of IVIG 0.8 g/kg over 6 hours as initial therapy because the platelet count is below 20 and the risk of intracranial haemorrhage is high enough to warrant first-line pharmacological intervention regardless of bleeding status
options.rationale: IVIG is reserved for major bleeding or planned surgery; platelet count alone (even below 20) is not an indication for pharmacological treatment in paediatric ITP. (Source: RCH CPG: Immune Thrombocytopenia)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 4 mg/kg/day for 4 days as a first-line treatment for paediatric immune thrombocytopenia, with tapering over the subsequent week and repeat platelet count at 1 week to assess response to corticosteroid therapy
options.rationale: Prednisolone is used for major bleeding or where treatment is required; it is not first-line for an asymptomatic well child with no bleeding even at platelet counts below 20. (Source: RCH CPG: Immune Thrombocytopenia)

options.text: Bone marrow examination before any management decision is made in order to exclude acute lymphoblastic leukaemia presenting with isolated thrombocytopenia, with treatment guided by bone marrow findings and haematologist input
options.rationale: Bone marrow examination is required only if atypical features (lymphadenopathy, organomegaly, abnormal blood film, abnormal red or white cell lines), none of which are present. (Source: RCH CPG: Immune Thrombocytopenia)

options.text: Single dose of intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg as inpatient therapy and admission for observation, with planned IVIG addition if no platelet response within 24 hours and haematology consultation for ongoing management
options.rationale: Pulse methylprednisolone is reserved for severe bleeding or refractory ITP; it is over-treatment of a well child without bleeding. (Source: RCH CPG: Immune Thrombocytopenia)

options.text: Observation only with no pharmacological treatment, written advice to avoid contact sport and NSAIDs, family education on signs of significant bleeding requiring re-presentation, and repeat platelet count by the GP in 1 week
options.rationale: Asymptomatic paediatric ITP without major bleeding is managed by observation; platelet count alone is not an indication for pharmacological therapy, and most acute cases resolve spontaneously within 3 months. (Source: RCH CPG: Immune Thrombocytopenia)

explanation.summary: Acute paediatric immune thrombocytopenia is a clinical diagnosis after viral illness with isolated thrombocytopenia and otherwise normal blood film. Treatment is guided by bleeding severity, NOT platelet count. Asymptomatic children without major bleeding are managed by observation with safety advice (avoid contact sport, NSAIDs, anticoagulants); most resolve spontaneously within 3 months. IVIG, anti-D, or prednisolone are reserved for major bleeding or planned procedures. Bone marrow examination is needed only when atypical features raise concern for malignancy.
key_points: Treat the bleeding, not the platelet count; observation if asymptomatic
Bone marrow only if atypical features (anaemia, abnormal WCC, lymphadenopathy, organomegaly)
Most acute paediatric ITP resolves within 3 months
explanation.pearls: Intracranial haemorrhage in paediatric ITP is very rare (<1%) even at very low platelet counts; head trauma, NSAIDs and anticoagulants are the main avoidable precipitants.
